subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940)
stimulus:
Newton Cantwell and his sons, who were Jehovah's Witnesses, went door-to-door in a New Haven, Connecticut, neighborhood to distribute pamphlets about their religion and encouraged people to join their church. The men did not have a permit to solicit door-to-door. A Connecticut state court convicted the Cantwells of violating an ordinance that stated "[n]o person shall solicit money, services, subscriptions or any valuable thing for any alleged religious, charitable or philanthropic cause. . . unless such cause shall have been approved by the secretary of the public welfare council."

Cantwell and his sons challenged the conviction, arguing that they did not need to obtain a permit because their activities were protected by the United States Constitution. The Connecticut Supreme Court upheld the conviction, finding that the statute was in the public interest and that it protected against fraud. The Cantwells appealed to the United States Supreme Court.

In a unanimous decision, the Supreme Court ruled in the Cantwells' favor, holding that the United States Constitution prohibited states from making laws like the one in question in New Haven. The Court reasoned that the First Amendment gives citizens the right to believe, as well as the right to act on those beliefs, and regarding the right to act, laws may not deny or limit the right to preach or disseminate religious views.

parts.prompt: Identify the First Amendment clause that is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) and Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the free exercise clause earns the point. The establishment clause does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the First Amendment clause that is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) and Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies the free exercise clause as the First Amendment clause common to both cases.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies the free exercise clause as the First Amendment clause common to both cases.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in the Supreme Court issuing similar holdings in both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Wisconsin v. Yoder. Second point requires connecting both cases through the common thread of the free exercise clause protecting religious practice from government restriction.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in the Supreme Court issuing similar holdings in both cases. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: In Yoder, mandatory public school attendance beyond the eighth grade was contrary to the religious beliefs of some Amish parents.
alternatives.correct_example: In Yoder, mandatory public school attendance beyond the eighth grade was contrary to the religious beliefs of some Amish parents.

required_elements: In Yoder, the Court held that exemptions from school attendance requirements for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause.
alternatives.correct_example: In Yoder, the Court held that exemptions from school attendance requirements for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in the Supreme Court issuing similar holdings in both cases. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: While Yoder was about school attendance and Cantwell was about solicitation, both cases concerned legal restrictions on religious practice. In both cases, the Court held that the laws were unconstitutional because they violated the free exercise clause.
alternatives.correct_example: While Yoder was about school attendance and Cantwell was about solicitation, both cases concerned legal restrictions on religious practice. In both cases, the Court held that the laws were unconstitutional because they violated the free exercise clause.

required_elements: In Yoder, parents claimed that a law requiring school past the eighth grade violated their religious beliefs. The Court held that exemptions from school for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause. In Cantwell, Jehovah’s Witnesses claimed that regulations on door-to-door solicitation were a restriction on a religious practice. The Court held that their solicitation, even without a permit, was also protected by the free exercise clause.
alternatives.correct_example: In Yoder, parents claimed that a law requiring school past the eighth grade violated their religious beliefs. The Court held that exemptions from school for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause. In Cantwell, Jehovah’s Witnesses claimed that regulations on door-to-door solicitation were a restriction on a religious practice. The Court held that their solicitation, even without a permit, was also protected by the free exercise clause.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts of Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) illustrate the Court's need to balance government power and the rights of citizens.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify both the government's legitimate interest (regulating solicitation/preventing fraud) and the individual right at stake (free exercise of religion) and explain how the Court balanced them.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts of Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) illustrate the Court's need to balance government power and the rights of citizens.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how the Cantwell facts illustrate the balance between government power and individual rights. Accept: in Cantwell, the Court had to balance the government's power to regulate door-to-door solicitation (a valid government interest) with the right of citizens to freely practice their religion (First Amendment free exercise).
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains how the Cantwell facts illustrate the balance between government power and individual rights. Accept: in Cantwell, the Court had to balance the government's power to regulate door-to-door solicitation (a valid government interest) with the right of citizens to freely practice their religion (First Amendment free exercise).
